one_line_takeaway: A malicious federated-learning server can reliably (>90% success, provably perfect true-positive rate) infer client-level membership in Federated Prompt-Tuning by injecting adversarial soft-prompt keys and watching which prompts get updated in a single round, and existing gradient-, output-, and outlier-detection-based MIA defenses all fail to stop it without severely damaging model accuracy.
key_finding: PROMPTMIA achieves near-perfect attack success rates at small batch sizes and maintains >90% ASR and Advantage even at larger batch sizes across all three ViT architectures and four dataset settings (paper claims >90% ASR as the headline empirical result), whereas the Naive Prompt Injection baseline collapses as batch size grows, reaching an Advantage of only approximately 20% on FourDataset at batch size 256. Against classical anomaly detection defenses (Table 1, averaged over all datasets/models): IsolationForest attains precision 0.2672 / recall 1.0000 / F1 0.4172 but does so by flagging nearly all benign keys as adversarial too (not a usable defense); LocalOutlierFactor detects essentially none of the injected adversarial keys (precision 0.0000 / recall 0.0000 / F1 0.0000); OneClassSVM achieves moderate recall with high false positives (precision 0.2038 / recall 0.4993 / F1 0.2851); EllipticEnvelope also fails to detect the attack (precision 0.0024 / recall 0.0052 / F1 0.0033) -- none of the four classical detectors provide a reliable, low-false-positive defense. Against input noise perturbation (local DP on ViT-B32, Table 2): at epsilon=3, clean model accuracy already drops substantially versus the non-DP baseline (e.g., CIFAR-10 0.85 vs 0.95 non-DP; CIFAR-100 0.50 vs 0.78 non-DP; TinyImageNet 0.72 vs 0.86 non-DP; FourDataset 0.55 vs 0.76 non-DP), and even at this cost the attack's ASR is only meaningfully suppressed at large batch sizes and strong (small-epsilon) privacy budgets -- e.g. CIFAR-100 at epsilon=3 shows a noticeable ASR decrease at larger batch sizes but at the cost of accuracy dropping from 0.78 (non-DP) to 0.50. At moderate privacy levels (epsilon=5, epsilon=8) the attack continues to achieve moderate-to-high ASR on three of four datasets even at large batch size. DPSGD (gradient-level DP) is argued analytically (not empirically, since deemed clearly ineffective) to be structurally ineffective because prompt selection is independent of the gradient update content it protects, so it was excluded from empirical evaluation. Ablations found ASR is notably higher on models trained for a few epochs than on randomly initialized prompt pools, consistent with the theoretical prediction that FPR falls as benign query clusters tighten around benign keys during training.
core_contribution: The paper introduces PROMPTMIA, an active membership inference attack (MIA) against Federated Prompt-Tuning (FPT), a federated learning paradigm where clients adapt a frozen pretrained Vision Transformer by selecting and updating a small pool of shared soft prompts (via a query-key mechanism, following Learning to Prompt / L2P). A malicious server injects adversarially crafted prompt-key pairs into the shared global prompt pool before a training round. These adversarial keys are constructed (via Algorithm 1/2, GENKEYWITHSIMILARITY and GENADVKEYSET) to have cosine similarity to a target sample's query feature that is guaranteed higher than any benign key's similarity by a margin delta_min, while also being spread across a controlled similarity band [smax+delta_min, smax+delta_min+Delta] so the adversarial keys are diverse rather than collapsing to an identical vector (the latter being the failure mode of a 'Naive Prompt Injection' baseline the authors define and analyze first). If the target sample belongs to a client's private dataset, the client's top-N query-key matching (Eq. 1) is provably guaranteed to select exactly the adversarial keys; the server then observes which prompts get updated in the returned model to infer membership in a single communication round, without needing gradients, full model weights, or shadow models. The authors formalize this as a security game (ExpAMI) between a challenger (client) and adversary (server), define attack Advantage and Attack Success Rate (ASR), prove the attack achieves True Positive Rate = 1 (Theorem 3.1), derive a probabilistic upper bound on False Positive Rate via a Gaussian cluster model of non-member queries around benign keys (Assumptions 1-2, Lemma 3.2, Theorem 3.3), and combine these into a lower bound on attack Advantage (Corollary 3.4). They then empirically validate PROMPTMIA against FPT and evaluate it under three families of defenses: classical unsupervised outlier/anomaly detection on the prompt pool, input noise perturbation (local differential privacy on pixel inputs), and (in the appendix, not fully captured here) system-level defenses such as randomized key indices, secure aggregation, and prompt dropout.
threat_model: Adversary: the federated learning server itself (an 'active' / malicious-server threat model, as opposed to a passive honest-but-curious server that merely observes updates). The server has full control over the contents of the global prompt-key pool it distributes to clients at the start of a round -- it can insert N adversarial key-prompt pairs, replacing entries in the pool, without clients ever inspecting individual key values by default. The attacker's goal is a binary membership inference: given a target sample T and a client's private local dataset D, determine whether T is in D. The attacker does NOT require access to client gradients, model weights, or an auxiliary/shadow dataset -- the only signal used is which of the N selected prompts get returned as updated after one communication round (a purely combinatorial/selection-based side channel, distinct from gradient- or output-based MIAs). The paper explicitly contrasts this attack surface with membership inference in in-context learning (ICL) prompting of LLMs (Wen et al., Duan et al.), noting FPT soft prompts do not embed raw private data and the adversary here is an insider (the server) rather than an external querying user, making prior ICL-privacy defenses inapplicable.
limitations: The paper's own stated scope limits: (1) it focuses specifically on Federated Prompt-Tuning (soft-prompt/query-key pool architectures like L2P); the authors explicitly note LoRA-based and adapter-based federated fine-tuning may exhibit similar vulnerabilities but were not evaluated, deemed 'architecturally orthogonal' and left untested. (2) The FPR bound (Theorem 3.3) rests on Assumptions 1-2 (benign keys as cluster centroids, Gaussian query distribution around them) that are empirically motivated via t-SNE/K-Means visualization rather than proven to hold generally; the paper itself frames this as 'a reasonable assumption' rather than a guarantee. (3) The evaluation of anomaly-detection defenses is restricted to four classical, non-deep-learning unsupervised methods; the authors explicitly state they leave deep-learning-based anomaly detectors (autoencoders, VAEs, GAN-based) for future work, reasoning (without testing) that the small prompt pool size (20 in their experiments) 'raises doubts' about deep methods' effectiveness there -- this is an unverified conjecture, not a result. (4) DPSGD is not empirically evaluated at all, only argued against analytically. (5) Defenses that modify the selection/aggregation protocol itself (randomized key indices, secure aggregation, prompt dropout) are discussed and claimed 'provably or empirically ineffective' but pushed to Appendix A.4, not present in the main-body text extracted here. (6) The datasets used (CIFAR variants, MNIST variants, TinyImageNet, a face-emotion dataset MMAFEDB) are all image classification benchmarks; generalization to genuinely sensitive real-world federated deployments (e.g., healthcare, finance, as invoked in the Impact Statement) is asserted as motivation but not empirically tested on such data in the pages reviewed. (7) The paper explicitly states its own goal is not to design new defenses -- it is an attack/vulnerability-disclosure paper, and it explicitly calls for future defense-focused research rather than providing one.

datasets_used: FourDataset (synthetic pooled benchmark of MNIST-M, Fashion-MNIST, CINIC-10, MMAFEDB)
quantitative_results: PROMPTMIA achieves >90% Attack Success Rate (ASR) and Advantage across all tested models/datasets at small batch sizes, remaining >90% at larger batch sizes
Naive Prompt Injection baseline Advantage collapses to approximately 20% on FourDataset at batch size 256

Outlier detection (Table 1, averaged over all datasets/models): IsolationForest precision 0.2672 / recall 1.0000 / F1 0.4172; LocalOutlierFactor precision 0.0000 / recall 0.0000 / F1 0.0000; OneClassSVM precision 0.2038 / recall 0.4993 / F1 0.2851; EllipticEnvelope precision 0.0024 / recall 0.0052 / F1 0.0033

Input noise perturbation accuracy trade-off (Table 2, ViT-B32): CIFAR-10 accuracy 0.85 (eps=3), 0.88 (eps=5), 0.90 (eps=8), 0.95 (non-DP); CIFAR-100 0.50 (eps=3), 0.59 (eps=5), 0.62 (eps=8), 0.78 (non-DP); TinyImageNet 0.72 (eps=3), 0.76 (eps=5), 0.79 (eps=8), 0.86 (non-DP); FourDataset 0.55 (eps=3), 0.59 (eps=5), 0.64 (eps=8), 0.76 (non-DP)
Theorem 3.1: True Positive Rate of PROMPTMIA = 1 (proven by construction)
Fixed experimental hyperparameters: global prompt pool size M=20, selection size N=4, delta_min=0.02, Delta=0.05, beta=0 (default)
baselines_compared: Naive Prompt Injection attack (adversary's own simpler baseline defined in Section 3.2.1, where all adversarial keys collapse to q(T) exactly)
Input noise perturbation / local differential privacy (epsilon in {3,5,8})
DPSGD (Differentially Private SGD) -- discussed analytically as ineffective, not empirically tested
